caveats: a: The occupational mix is a broad durable-goods proxy rather than a six-digit observation.
a: O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage share, AI feasibility, or current automation.
a: The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a stated harmonization factor.
rho: Survey respondents and examples are broader and generally larger than the target population.
rho: Sales opportunity and management productivity are not equivalent to labor substitution.
rho: Implementation likely differs sharply between multi-branch systems and locally managed yards.
e: No public source directly measures default-lens eligibility within the injected 422-firm estimate.
e: The injected count is inferred from receipts size classes and a sector margin rather than observed EBITDA.
e: The code mixes masonry products, aggregates, cement-related goods, asphalt, and concrete products with different logistics and repeat-service patterns.
s5: Plans may not close and can include gifts, minority transactions, or internal succession.
s5: McKinsey's M&A evidence is dominated by large distribution transactions.
s5: Real estate, environmental liabilities, permits, cyclicality, and fleet or yard capital can impede transfer.
q: No direct study measures five-year pass-through of AI-derived savings in this industry.
q: Fuel, freight, commodity costs, inventory timing, and breakage can obscure the economic effect of labor savings.
q: This estimate excludes implementation probability and demand-volume effects.
d5: Employment is an input measure rather than constant-price, constant-quality service-basket demand.
d5: Infrastructure authorization, obligation, outlay, project construction, and wholesaler sales occur on different schedules.
d5: Product price inflation, local project cycles, direct quarry or producer sales, and channel share can separate spending from wholesale physical quantity.
o: Distribution-wide bypass evidence may overstate substitution for heavy local materials.
o: Operator need is lower for full-truck or full-rail producer-direct movements than for mixed, staged, short-notice local orders.
o: The estimate separates operator requirement from total construction demand and implementation difficulty.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Brick, Stone, and Related Construction Material Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product sales to masonry, concrete, paving, civil, landscape, residential, and commercial contractors and dealers; the wholesaler earns merchandise margin while providing local availability, credit, estimating or product support, load coordination, and jobsite delivery.
lens.excluded_activities: Quarries, mines, manufacturers including ready-mix concrete producers, manufacturers' sales branches, pure brokers and commission agents, consumer-only retailers, captive internal supply units, shells, and operations that cannot transfer independently of the owner.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers that repeatedly source, inventory, quote, sell, dispatch, and support stone, cement, lime, sand, gravel, brick, asphalt and concrete mixtures, and concrete, stone, or structural-clay products for external construction customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for a broader durable-goods wholesaler group that includes NAICS 4233, and O*NET describes nontechnical wholesale-sales tasks rather than brick-and-stone distribution. I mapped quoting, order entry, service, account research, pricing support, purchasing, dispatch communication, and inventory analysis to partial AI exposure, then conservatively wage-weighted against heavy physical handling, equipment, driving, field sales, estimating, and management. Bulk-material logistics make the broad proxy likely to overstate exposure.
caveats: The occupational mix is a broad durable-goods proxy rather than a six-digit observation.
O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage share, AI feasibility, or current automation.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a stated harmonization factor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study of target-band masonry and aggregate distributors, separated across inside sales, outside sales, estimating, purchasing, dispatch, yard, equipment, delivery, finance, and management, would materially revise exposure.
a.rationale: Sales, service, inventory, and office workflows contain substantial language and information processing, but the broader group also has major material-moving, stocking, driving, and equipment occupations. Product selection, quantity and site judgment, negotiation, yard operations, dispatch, and delivery remain human-intensive.

caveats: Employment is an input measure rather than constant-price, constant-quality service-basket demand.
Infrastructure authorization, obligation, outlay, project construction, and wholesaler sales occur on different schedules.
Product price inflation, local project cycles, direct quarry or producer sales, and channel share can separate spending from wholesale physical quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Deflated six-digit sales or physical-tonnage data split among aggregate, cement, asphalt, masonry, and concrete products, paired with five-year residential, nonresidential, highway, bridge, water, and public-works forecasts, would materially revise d5.
d5.rationale: Recent BLS employment observations show a softer but still sizable industry footprint. Housing and multifamily weakness constrain near-term volume, while residential remodeling, eventual single-family improvement, civil infrastructure projects already obligated but not fully outlayed, repair backlogs, and the essential use of aggregate, cement, masonry, and concrete products support five-year quantity.

caveats: No public source directly measures default-lens eligibility within the injected 422-firm estimate.
The injected count is inferred from receipts size classes and a sector margin rather than observed EBITDA.
The code mixes masonry products, aggregates, cement-related goods, asphalt, and concrete products with different logistics and repeat-service patterns.
e.change_evidence: A representative target-band file with invoice repeat rates, customer and supplier concentration, owner hours, property and permit status, fleet and yard economics, credit exposure, and standalone earnings would establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Contractors and dealers often purchase repeatedly from local material suppliers, and inventory, yards, fleet relationships, credit processes, staff, and supplier access can transfer. Eligibility is reduced by transactional commodity traders, project-only sellers, owner-held contractor accounts, captive channels, customer or supplier concentration, and operations whose permits, sites, or reputation are not readily transferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423320 ($5,461,552,691) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423320 ($38,874,607,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $988,245 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423320); class <5: 1,109 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,334,623 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $151,747 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 451 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,432,490 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $503,974 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 306 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,064,546 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $916,939 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 323 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,494,211 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,126,092 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $39,035,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,438,355 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $296,802,132 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,746,402 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 422

caveats: Distribution-wide bypass evidence may overstate substitution for heavy local materials.
Operator need is lower for full-truck or full-rail producer-direct movements than for mixed, staged, short-notice local orders.
The estimate separates operator requirement from total construction demand and implementation difficulty.
o.change_evidence: Contractor and producer surveys measuring direct purchasing, marketplace use, automated replenishment, drop shipment, haul arrangements, and distributor bypass by product and order size, combined with branch-level retention cohorts, would move o.
o.rationale: The basket is physical, heavy, locally constrained, and often time-sensitive. Contractors need accountable sourcing, product and quantity support, credit, yard availability, load coordination, site-compatible delivery, and resolution of shortages or damage. Digital ordering and producer-direct full loads can bypass the distributor for some standardized demand, but software cannot perform the bulk logistics.

caveats: No direct study measures five-year pass-through of AI-derived savings in this industry.
Fuel, freight, commodity costs, inventory timing, and breakage can obscure the economic effect of labor savings.
This estimate excludes implementation probability and demand-volume effects.
q.change_evidence: Customer-, job-, and SKU-level contribution margins before and after automation, matched to quotes, bid outcomes, haul recovery, rebates, returns, breakage, and competitor pricing, would directly measure retained benefit.
q.rationale: Own-account product resale means labor savings initially remain inside gross margin. Contractor bidding, commodity reference prices, haul competition, national consolidators, supplier pressure, and digital transparency pass through part of the gain, while scarce local stock, reliable dispatch, credit, accurate quantities, mixed loads, and jobsite performance protect some retention.

rho.bridge: McKinsey surveys distributors across industries and cites large construction-materials operators, not target-band brick, stone, and aggregate wholesalers. I use its adoption-versus-realized-savings gap and construction-material examples to bound five-year implementation, then haircut for small-firm data, ERP, capital, integration, and change limits. Evidence from scaled distributors likely overstates implementation for the lens.
caveats: Survey respondents and examples are broader and generally larger than the target population.
Sales opportunity and management productivity are not equivalent to labor substitution.
Implementation likely differs sharply between multi-branch systems and locally managed yards.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal cohort of comparable distributors measuring labor hours, error rates, quote turnaround, adoption, and verified savings before and after AI and ERP deployments, including failed projects, would materially move rho.
rho.rationale: Quote assistance, order intake, account prioritization, pricing guidance, purchasing, demand alerts, delivery-status communication, and administration are implementable within five years. Variable units, site and haul constraints, negotiated prices, legacy systems, product-master quality, credit, safety, and human accountability for schedules and quantities limit realization.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans among all US employer-business owners, while McKinsey documents broader distribution and building-products M&A, not completed qualifying transfers for NAICS 423320. I anchor near Gallup's 22% employer sale-or-transfer intention, reduce for failed, internal, partial, or nontransferable outcomes, and allow for unplanned sales and active strategic demand. Intentions likely overstate qualifying completions.
caveats: Plans may not close and can include gifts, minority transactions, or internal succession.
McKinsey's M&A evidence is dominated by large distribution transactions.
Real estate, environmental liabilities, permits, cyclicality, and fleet or yard capital can impede transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Industry-specific owner age and tenure joined to five years of SBA 7(a), broker, PitchBook, Capital IQ, trade-publication, and private-company control-transfer records would materially tighten s5.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner plans imply a meaningful transfer pool and building-products distribution has visible transaction interest. However, large disclosed strategic deals do not supply a denominator or establish the transfer rate for lower-middle-market local material wholesalers.

principal_driver: A high compensation input combines with recurring quote, order, purchasing, pricing, credit, and dispatch workflows around indispensable physical materials.
principal_weakness: Most labor and operational risk remain physical, local, and safety-critical, limiting the share of wages AI can actually remove.
